paragraphs: Alstonville is a selected, named cultivar of the finger lime (Citrus australasica), the thorny rainforest citrus native to the coastal border region of Queensland and New South Wales. It is one of the green-fleshed selections and was among the earliest finger limes available commercially, grown grafted for reliable cropping.

The cylindrical fruit has dark green to greenish-black skin and pale green flesh made up of caviar-like juice vesicles (pearls) that burst with a tangy, lime-like flavour. The pearls are mostly seedless and prized as a fresh garnish and in preserves.

claims.text: Finger lime is Citrus australasica, a thorny rainforest citrus native to the Queensland and New South Wales border region
Alstonville is a named, selected green-fleshed cultivar of finger lime, grown grafted
Dark green to greenish-black skin with pale green flesh
Fruit holds caviar-like juice vesicles (pearls) that burst with a tangy, lime-like flavour
Pearls are mostly seedless and used fresh as a garnish and in preserves

Sustainable Gardening Australia: Finger Limes

paragraphs: Byron Sunrise is a named Australian finger lime cultivar held in the UC Riverside citrus collection, where it is recorded as one of a group of finger lime cultivars selected by Judy Viola (or colleagues) and used as a parent for further open-pollinated selections such as Rubirosa and Ruby My Dear.

It is grown chiefly for its colour: nursery sources consistently describe dark maroon skin and bright red to orange-red pearls, the sunrise tones that give the selection its name. The flesh is reported as comparatively mild and low in acidity, which suits it as an ornamental garnish for seafood, desserts and drinks.

claims.text: Byron Sunrise is a named finger lime cultivar selected by Judy Viola or colleagues and is held at the UC Riverside citrus collection, where it is a parent of seedling selections such as Rubirosa.
It is described as having dark maroon skin and bright red to orange-red juice pearls.
The flesh is reported as comparatively mild with low acidity.

sources.name: Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection, UC Riverside - Rubirosa finger lime (CRC4296)
Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection, UC Riverside - finger lime (CRC4295)
Daleys Fruit - Grafted Finger Lime Byron Sunrise

paragraphs: Chartreuse stands apart from the red and pink finger limes on colouring alone. The fruit is green skinned with pale green to yellow flesh and clear to lime green vesicles. The flavour is a subtle, tangy citrus that is widely described as less bitter than other finger lime varieties, which makes it one of the gentler selections to eat fresh and a popular pick for salads, seafood and desserts.
Nurseries list an extended flowering and fruiting season for Chartreuse, with harvest running from about March to August, a longer window than many single-flush finger lime selections offer.

claims.text: Green fruit with pale green to yellow flesh and clear to lime green vesicles
Subtle citrus flavour, described as less bitter than other finger lime varieties
Extended flowering and fruiting season, with harvest listed from about March to August

sources.name: Aussie Green Thumb, Citrus australasica Finger Limes Growing Guide
Princess Fancy Plants, Finger Lime Chartreuse
Fruit Tree Lane, Chartreuse Finger Lime Trees

paragraphs: D'Emerald is a black-skinned finger lime whose almost black fruit conceals bright emerald green pulp, a striking colour contrast that makes it one of the most recognisable selections in the Australian trade. The juicy citrus pearls are noted for being unusually easy to extract from the fruit. Cropping peaks in autumn, with lighter fruiting continuing through much of the year. It is sold grafted in the Citrus Gems range of native citrus, including a dwarf form suited to pots and hedging, and some nurseries also list it under the name Fat Black.

The name is generally treated as a short trade form of Durham's Emerald, among the finger lime cultivars registered with the Australian Cultivar Registration Authority, which carries the same description: a medium shrub bearing black fruit with emerald green, easy-to-extract vesicles, cropping heavily from summer to late autumn with continual light cropping year round. That registered cultivar arose in the Northern Rivers district of New South Wales and was introduced into cultivation in 1994.

claims.text: Fruit has black to very dark skin with emerald green pulp
Vesicles are easy to extract from the fruit
Fruits mainly in autumn with continued lighter cropping through the year
Sold grafted in the Citrus Gems native citrus range, including a dwarf form; also listed by some nurseries as Fat Black

Durham's Emerald is an ACRA-registered cultivar described as a medium open shrub with black fruit and emerald green pulp, originating in the Northern Rivers district and introduced into cultivation in 1994

paragraphs: The listing name D Emerald maps to D'Emerald, a selected cultivar of the Australian native finger lime (Citrus australasica). It is grown for slender, finger shaped fruit with notably dark, almost black skin, which sets it apart from the green and red skinned finger lime selections.

Cut open, the fruit releases bright emerald green juice vesicles (the citrus caviar) that pop with a fresh lime tang and lift easily for use in cooking. Fruit ripens mainly in autumn, though the plant tends to keep bearing through much of the year, and the bush has a compact, dense habit that suits pots, hedging, or a culinary feature plant.

claims.text: D'Emerald (listed as D Emerald) is a selected cultivar of the Australian native finger lime, Citrus australasica.
Its finger shaped fruit has dark, almost black skin.
Inside the fruit are bright emerald green juice vesicles (citrus caviar).
Fruit ripens mainly in autumn, with the plant continuing to bear through much of the year.
The plant has a compact, dense habit suited to pots or hedging.

sources.name: Gardening With Angus, Citrus australasica 'D'Emerald'
Nursery Warehouse, Citrus australasica 'D-Emerald' Emerald Finger Lime

paragraphs: Giant Jali Red is the trade name nurseries use for Jali Red, a finger lime cultivar registered with the Australian Cultivar Registration Authority in 2010 after being selected by grower Judy Viola at Whian Whian in the Northern Rivers district of New South Wales. The 'giant' refers to the fruit: nurseries promote it as one of the largest finger limes available, and the official registration describes cylindrical fruit up to 8cm long.

The fruit has dark brown to maroon skin filled with red to pink caviar pearls, and the registration notes that the distinctive red and pink flesh darkens after several hours of exposure to air, so it is best used fresh. Plants form an open shrub or small tree of 2 to 3 metres, flowering white in late winter (July to August), and nurseries describe the flavour as a tangy citrus burst suited to garnishes, sauces and gourmet dishes.

claims.text: Jali Red is registered with the Australian Cultivar Registration Authority (registered 2010), applicant Judy Viola, originating at Whian Whian in northern New South Wales
Fruit is cylindrical, 40 to 80mm long, with dark brown skin and red to pink vesicles
The red/pink flesh darkens when exposed to air for several hours
Marketed by nurseries as Giant Jali Red for its extra-large fruit
Open-habit tall shrub or small tree to 2 to 3 metres, with white flowers in July to August
Flavour described as a tangy citrus burst, used in garnishes, sauces and gourmet dishes

sources.name: Australian Cultivar Registration Authority, Citrus australasica 'Jali Red'

paragraphs: Green Sapphire is a green-fruited finger lime selection that keeps the classic lime look at both ends: slender, dark green fruit filled with vivid green, caviar-like pearls. Most named finger lime cultivars chase dark skins and pink or red pulp, so a deliberately green selection stands out among them. White flowers appear in spring and the fruit ripens mainly in autumn.

It is sold as part of the Citrus Gems range of Australian native citrus selections and is noted for an unusually graceful habit for a finger lime, with willowy, lightly weeping branches and soft, pale foliage rather than the typical stiff thorny bush. It suits containers as well as garden planting, which has made it one of the more common cultivars in general garden centres.

claims.text: Green-skinned fruit filled with vivid green, caviar-like pearls
Willowy, lightly weeping habit with soft, light coloured foliage
Flowers in spring; fruit ripens mainly in autumn
Sold as part of the Citrus Gems range of Australian native citrus selections
Suited to container growing as well as garden planting

Nursery Warehouse, Green Sapphire Finger Lime
Flower Power, Citrus Gems Green Sapphire Finger Lime

paragraphs: Jali Red is one of the few finger lime cultivars formally registered with the Australian Cultivar Registration Authority. It originated at Whian Whian in the Northern Rivers region of New South Wales, in the heart of the finger lime's native range, with the registration (applicant Judy Viola) accepted in 2005 and formalised in 2010.

The fruit is a cylindrical berry with dark brown to maroon skin enclosing pink to red caviar pearls, and the ACRA description notes the red of the vesicles deepens once exposed to air. The plant is a tall shrub or small tree of around 2 to 3 metres with a sparse open habit, white flowers in July and August, and moderate frost hardiness. In the nursery trade Daleys rates it as one of the largest fruited finger limes and a high yielding variety with good flavour, harvested from March through September.

claims.text: Originated at Whian Whian in northern New South Wales and registered with the Australian Cultivar Registration Authority (application 2005 by Judy Viola, registered 2010)
Cylindrical fruit with dark brown to maroon skin and pink to red vesicles
Vesicle colour deepens when exposed to air
Tall shrub or small tree to about 2 to 3 metres with a sparse open habit, white flowers in July and August, and moderate frost hardiness
Described by Daleys as one of the largest finger limes and a high yielding variety with good flavour, harvested March through September

sources.name: Australian Cultivar Registration Authority, Citrus australasica 'Jali Red'
Daleys Fruit Tree Nursery, Finger Lime Jali Red

paragraphs: Judy's Everbearing is a prolific finger lime selected by Judy Viola (or colleagues). Its name reflects an unusually long cropping habit: it carries a heavy crop from summer to autumn and produces fruit sporadically through the rest of the year, where most finger limes fruit in a single tighter window. The dark fruit holds clear, light green to pink pearls with few seeds.

It is one of the cultivars used in peer-reviewed chemotype work, where its peel oil was characterised by a distinctive limonene, citronellal and isomenthone profile, a combination not previously reported in other citrus. The aromatic lemon-lime pearls are used as for any finger lime, with seafood, in drinks, or in place of lemon and lime.

claims.text: Judy's Everbearing was selected by Judy Viola or colleagues.
It crops heavily from summer to autumn and also produces fruit sporadically through the year, an everbearing habit.
The pearls are clear, light green to pink with few seeds.
Its peel oil has a distinctive limonene, citronellal and isomenthone chemotype not previously reported in other citrus.

The aromatic lemon-lime pearls are used as for any finger lime, with seafood, in drinks, or in place of lemon and lime.

paragraphs: Mt White is the odd one out among nursery finger limes. It is not a Citrus australasica selection at all, but Citrus garrawayi, the Mount White lime, a closely related Australian native citrus from tropical rainforest on Cape York Peninsula in far north Queensland. In the wild it grows as a rainforest understorey tree and is rare, and the species has never been commercially cropped, so a garden plant is essentially a collector's item.

The fruit reads as a shorter, thicker finger lime, with green to yellowish-green skin and green caviar style pulp vesicles, and the tree is easy to tell from true finger limes by its noticeably broader leaves. It can reach around 15 metres in its rainforest habitat but stays much smaller in cultivation. The pearls are edible and used the same way as finger lime, fresh over seafood or in cooking, with the appeal here being rarity rather than colour range.

claims.text: Sold under finger lime listings but is actually Citrus garrawayi, the Mount White lime, a distinct species often placed in Microcitrus
Native to rainforest on Cape York Peninsula, far north Queensland, where it grows as an understorey tree; rare in the wild with no commercial cultivation to date
Fruit is shorter and thicker than finger lime, with green to yellowish-green skin and green pulp vesicles
Has noticeably broader leaves than true finger limes
Can reach about 15 metres in its natural habitat but remains much smaller in cultivation
Fruit is edible and used like finger lime, fresh or in cooking, but the species has not been commercialised

paragraphs: Pink Ice is one of the first commercialised finger lime varieties. It produces red to burgundy coloured fruit with clear to pink pearls of juice inside, giving a striking colour contrast when the fruit is opened.

Its distinctive draw is aroma: the pearls carry a scent likened to ruby grapefruit, which makes Pink Ice popular for cocktails, drinks and desserts as well as savoury dishes. Fruit is borne from summer to autumn and is ready when it comes away easily from the branch.

claims.text: Pink Ice was one of the first commercialised finger lime varieties.
The fruit is red to burgundy coloured with clear to pink juice pearls inside.
The aroma of the pearls is similar to ruby grapefruit.
Fruit is borne from summer to autumn and is ripe when it comes away easily from the branch.

sources.name: Gardening With Angus - Citrus australasica 'Pink Ice'
Garden Express - Finger Lime Pink Ice

paragraphs: Rainforest Pearl is a pink-fleshed finger lime classified as Citrus australasica var. sanguinea, selected for its pink pulp, ease of propagation and ornamental garden value. The aromatic, finger-shaped fruit has skin that is green tinged with red, and inside sit small pearls of tangy juice with a pink cast.

Fruit is produced in autumn (late summer to autumn on the NSW north coast). Plants are usually grafted to make them hardier and more tolerant of frost and dry conditions, forming an upright tree. The tangy pearls suit seafood, Asian cooking and cocktails.

claims.text: Rainforest Pearl is a pink-pulped selection classified as Citrus australasica var. sanguinea, chosen for its pink pulp and ornamental qualities.
The aromatic skin is green tinged with red and the fruit is finger shaped.
The juice pearls are pink and tangy, suiting seafood, Asian cooking and cocktails.
Fruit is produced in autumn.
Plants are grafted to improve hardiness and tolerance of frost and dry conditions.

sources.name: Gardening With Angus - Citrus australasica var. sanguinea 'Rainforest Pearl'
Australian Native Plants Society - Citrus australasica

paragraphs: Red Champagne is one of the most popular and widely grown finger lime selections in Australia, taken up by commercial orchards for its flavour and its unusually long fruiting season, roughly January to May. The fruit ripens to a dark maroon to red skin, and the pearls inside range from pale and clear through pink to red. The taste is milder and subtly sweeter than green finger limes, often described as sitting somewhere between grapefruit and Tahitian lime.

It also handles life after harvest unusually well. Growers rate it as having one of the longest shelf lives of any finger lime variety, and postharvest research cited in a Nuffield Australia report found Red Champagne pearls held their condition best in cold storage at 4 degrees Celsius, which helps explain its standing with chefs and commercial growers.

claims.text: Dark maroon to red skin with pearls ranging from pale or clear through pink to red
Mild, subtly sweet flavour often compared to grapefruit and Tahitian lime
Long fruiting season, roughly January to May
A select commercial variety and one of the most popular finger limes grown in Australia
Long shelf life; postharvest research found its pearls kept condition best at 4 degrees Celsius

paragraphs: Sunrise Lime is not a straight finger lime selection but a CSIRO bred native citrus hybrid, a seedling selection of a faustrimedin, the cross between the finger lime and a calamondin (itself a mandarin and cumquat hybrid). It was granted Plant Breeders Rights in 2004 alongside CSIRO's Australian Blood and Outback limes and is licensed to nurseries across Australia.

The fruit looks nothing like typical finger lime caviar: it is small, pear shaped and golden to orange skinned, with a sweet edible rind and pith around tangy juice that carries a delicate floral note, so it can be eaten whole like a cumquat. Fruit comes on through the cooler months, with availability running roughly February to June, and it is especially valued for marmalade, jams and preserves.

claims.text: CSIRO developed hybrid, a seedling selection of a faustrimedin, the cross between the finger lime and a calamondin (a mandarin and cumquat hybrid)
Granted Plant Breeders Rights in 2004 alongside the Australian Blood and Outback limes and licensed to several nurseries across Australia
Small pear shaped fruit with golden to orange skin
Sweet edible rind and pith with tangy juice and a delicate floral note; can be eaten whole like a cumquat
Valued for marmalade, jams and preserves
Fruits through the cooler months, available roughly February to June
